entries.title: About the Course
entries.content: Get to know the diverse people who inhabited North America before the European explorers arrived. Learn how they used what was available around them for food, shelter, clothing, and protection. Observe their customs, hear their stories, and discover how they pass on their traditional knowledge today.

rows.label: History: Grade 1 (Canada)
rows.books: The Honour Drum||On the Trapline||Hiawatha and the Peacemaker||Thirteen Moons on Turtle's Back: A Native American Year of Moons||Maple Moon||The Pencil||The Gift of the Great Buffalo

entries.content:
Permission to print for non-commercial use. See Alveary group use policy to use lessons
in a group context.

LINKS: Click text or scan the QR code in the top corner of the lesson plan pages to view
online resources associated with the lessons.

Responsibility for previewing all links rests with the teacher. All links were checked at the
time of publication; however, websites change frequently and may contain objectionable
content. Please report broken links by contacting us through our website.

books.title: Thirteen Moons on Turtle's Back: A Native American Year of Moons
The Gift of the Great Buffalo

blocks.text:
• Read through the lesson and gather any materials or find any necessary links. 

• If you have not already read the book, you may want to read the selected pages in advance.

• Connect to previous lessons by asking students to recall where they left off. 

• If students have difficulty recalling, teachers might try showing them a picture from the reading, sharing something the teacher remembers, or prompting them with the first sentence of the last reading. This will often refresh their memory enough to allow them to recall.

• If students still cannot recall anything from the last reading, teachers might consider continuing anyway (with a shorter reading this time) or repeating the previous lesson, depending on the reading and the nature of the book.

• Use a picture, map, or engaging question to pique the student's interest in the day's lesson. 

• If necessary, you may talk about one or two unfamiliar words that will be crucial to understanding.

• Read the day's passage out loud. When reading to pre-readers, point to the words as you read, even if it seems like they aren't paying attention.

• Move at the student’s pace for the lesson time rather than a particular number of pages. It is vitally important that teachers not try to ‘force feed’ a student more than they can consume in one sitting.

• Students who have a difficult time sitting still or listening may benefit from a fidget of their choice, such as a piece of putty or clay or an afghan; flexible positioning, such as a wobble seat or inverted sitting; or sensory modulation, such as a weighted vest or blanket or adjustment of light, temperature, or sound.

• Have students retell the passage orally.

• Alternatively, learners may use words, pictures, Legos, dolls, etc., to process and convey ideas in the manner most natural to them.  

• Teachers may take turns to model.

• Narration is a complex skill requiring the coordination of several parts of the brain. If a student struggles to narrate in general, with a specific subject or book, or on one day but not others, then this is an opportunity to learn. Observe when they struggle with this or any other activity today. Notice facial expressions and body language; listen to what they say about why it is hard or how they feel about it. Take note of these observations for later, tell them that you see how hard they are working, and then offer whatever support they need to end on a positive note. If necessary, take a break for the day.

• Give the student an opportunity to share thoughts on the lesson and any connections made.  

• To stimulate discussion, you could ask them what they thought about the passage or ask an open-ended question.

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (Canada) - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
Turtle Island
⍞ Materials: The Honour Drum; globe, atlas, or map of Canada

➜ INTRO
What do you know about your own family's history in Canada? Where did your ancestors come from? Where did they settle? What kinds of homes might they have lived in? Where in Canada do you live now? 

➜ LOCATE
Use a globe, atlas, or map as a visual aid as you discuss these questions.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.2-3 "Look to the people" - "North American Culture."

• You can use Discussion Question #1 (p.3) in your discussion of the reading. We will return to the other Discussion Questions on this page in Term 3.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• ILLUSTRATE - Draw one of the kinds of dwellings from the reading using watercolour or another medium such as chalk, pastels, or crayons.

• EXTRA HELPING
Listen to a retelling of the Legend of Turtle Island for young children by Joel Agowissa, an Indigenous Support Worker with the Sudbury Catholic District School Board. This would make for a great activity during lunchtime or the afternoon.
∞ Video Link: The Ojibwe Legend of Turtle Island

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (Canada) - Lesson 2
lessons.body:
Keeping Time: Moons 
⍞ Materials: Thirteen Moons on Turtle's Back; the current month's calendar, showing the phases of the moon

➜ INTRO
How many months are in a year? Can you say them all? Indigenous people had their way of marking time using what they observed in nature. One "moon" was the time from one full moon to the next. How many moons do you think there are in a year? Look at a calendar for this month. What day is it today? When is the next full moon? What do you think the moon will look like tonight?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
1\. Read the final page - "A note about this book" - as an introduction to the various ways the native people of North America used nature's signs to tell time. 
2\. Look at the image of the turtle's back on the opening page and count the scales together.
3\. Read the first two sections from "Grandfather leaned over" - "keep us warm."
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER NOTE
For the remainder of Term 1, lessons will include one or two poems from Thirteen Moons on Turtle's Back. As a fun challenge, encourage your students to guess what time of year the poem describes best.

• EXTRA HELPING
Make your own full moon turtle calendar.
∞ Link: Turtle Lunar Calendar Craft Template

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (Canada) - Lesson 5
lessons.body:
The Owl and the Lemming: Trickster Tales
⍞ Materials: Thirteen Moons

➜ INTRO
This is a "trickster tale" from the Eskimo/Inuit people. Many cultures have these kinds of stories. In the video, you will hear Inuit language and music. See if you can figure out why this kind of story is called a "trickster tale."

➜ VIEW & NARRATE
∞ Video Link: The Owl and the Lemming 

➜ RECAP
What do you remember from what we read last time?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
"Frog Moon" (fourth moon): "When the world" - "with their name."

• What role does Wis-a-ked-jak play in the poem?
lessons.teacherNotes: • ILLUSTRATE - Draw a scene from the story using watercolor or another medium such as chalk, pastels, or crayons.

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (Canada) - Lesson 6

Dance
⍞ Materials: Thirteen Moons; The Honour Drum

➜ RECAP
What do you remember from what we read last time?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
"Budding Moon" (fifth moon): "One year" - "beautiful land."

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.8-9 "Dances of meaning" - "need healing."

• Questions #1-3 on p.9 can guide your discussion of the reading.

➜ VIEW & DISCUSS
Below are links to several short videos of different types of Indigenous dances. If you don't have enough time during the lesson, you could choose one or two as an afternoon listening activity.

• What do you notice about the style of dance, the regalia the dancers wear, or what they hold in their hands? 

∞ Video Link: Raven Sutherland jingle dress dance 
∞ Video Link: Shanley Spence - hoop dance 
∞ Video Link: Men's fancy dance 
∞ Video Link (optional, since it is longer): Celebrating through dance

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (Canada) - Lesson 9
lessons.body:
Life in the Arctic
⍞ Materials: Thirteen Moons; The Pencil

➜ RECAP
What do you remember from what we read last time?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
"Moon of Falling Leaves" (tenth moon): "Long ago" - "to the earth."
"Moon When Deer Drop Their Horns" (eleventh moon): "Now is the time" - "peaceful snow."

➜ INTRO
How easy is it for you to go to a store when you run out of something your family needs? Can you walk? Do you need to drive? How long is the drive? Imagine living somewhere far away from cities and stores. What would happen if you ran out of something?
The Pencil is a story based on Susan Avingaq's own childhood growing up in the Arctic and living in an iglu with her family. Through this joyful story, Avingaq shows the importance of good stewardship, using what we have wisely, and the delight that can be found in simple objects.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.31 "Inuktut Pronunciation Guide"
Review the Inuktut words before the reading with your student(s).
p.2-7 "'See you later!'" - "even a pencil!"

• Look closely at the pictures. How is Susan's home different from yours? How is it the same?
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER TIP
Students should also be encouraged to narrate using Legos, puppets, or by acting out the scene.

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (Canada) - Lesson 10
lessons.body:
Life in the Arctic, cont.
⍞ Materials: Thirteen Moons; The Pencil

➜ RECAP
What do you remember from what we read last time?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
"Moon When Wolves Run Together" (twelfth moon): "Long ago" - "we are together."

• Think about what is happening around you in nature during this time of the year. If you were going to name the full moon this month after something you've observed in nature, what would it be? Could you tell a short story or a simple poem about it?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.31 "Inuktut Pronunciation Guide"
Review the Inuktut words before the reading with your student(s).
p.8-17 "'The sun's out!'" - "for your brother?"

• What are some of the ways Susan and her siblings spend their time?

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (Canada) - Lesson 11
lessons.body:
Life in the Arctic, cont.
⍞ Materials: Thirteen Moons; The Pencil

➜ RECAP
What do you remember from what we read last time?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
"Big Moon" (thirteenth moon): "The elders say" - "Old Turtle's back."

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.31 "Inuktut Pronunciation Guide"
If necessary, review the Inuktut words before the reading with your student(s).
p.18-29 "I couldn't believe it!" - "what we did have."

• How did Susan think her mother would react when she got home? Why do you think she reacted the way she did?
lessons.teacherNotes:
• NATURE NOTEBOOK
Go on a nature walk this week and look for interesting plants that grow in your area. Make an illustration in your nature notebook during your walk or when you return home.

➜ EXAMS

If a student has difficulty getting started or gets "stuck," they may look back at illustrations to help them refocus on ideas.

➜ VIEW

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (Canada) - Lesson 17
lessons.body:
Storytelling: Maple Syrup
⍞ Materials: Maple Moon; a globe or map of North America

➜ INTRO
Think of a forest or nature area near where you live. Imagine you lived there a long time ago, before there were cities and towns as we know them now. What would you need to survive through the winter? Where could you find food? How could you keep yourself warm and sheltered?

➜ LOCATE
Find Rice Lake - southeast of Peterborough, Ontario - on a map or globe. Some Mississauga First Nation families made their homes here before people came from Europe. Do you live near here?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
"A long time ago" - "watched all of this."
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER TIP
Students should also be encouraged to narrate using Legos, puppets, or by acting out the scene.

lessons.body:

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (Canada) - Lesson 20
lessons.body:
Métis Culture and the Buffalo Hunt
⍞ Materials: The Gift of the Great Buffalo; any map or atlas showing North America

➜ INTRO
So far this term, we've learned about First Nations and Inuit peoples. But there is one more group of Indigenous people in Canada: the Métis. The word Métis, from a French adjective meaning "mixed," refers to people who have both Indigenous and European ancestry. The next book we read, The Gift of the Great Buffalo, describes the Métis way of life on the prairie through the eyes of a young girl whose family gathers with many other families for a buffalo hunt.

➜ LOCATE
In this book, the buffalo hunt takes place in Pembina, North Dakota. Use the map in the Extra Helpings link if you do not have an atlas.
Can you find Pembina, ND? What is it close to? (the U.S.-Canada border) The area was part of the Red River Settlement, which over the course of the nineteenth century became home to a vibrant Métis culture and very important to the history of Manitoba.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
1\. Read "Important Terms" on the second last page of the book to familiarize yourself and your student(s) with the Ojibwe words.
2\. This book has no page numbers. Read from "Pulling the buffalo robes tighter" - "better than the last."

• Looking closely at the illustrations, what do you think Rose's life is like? What are iron horses, and how do you think they and the settlers have reduced the number of buffalo?
lessons.teacherNotes:
• EXTRA HELPING
Learn more about the importance of Red River carts in Métis culture. This link has a photo of a Métis family camping with the cart in 1872, and a map of the cart trails through the prairies and the Red River Settlement, including Pembina.
∞ Link: Red River Carts

Métis Culture and the Buffalo Hunt, cont.
⍞ Materials: The Gift of the Great Buffalo

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS

• What do you think of Rose's decision? What do you think she is going to do?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS

• ILLUSTRATE - Draw a scene from today's reading using watercolour or another medium such as chalk, pastels, pencils, or crayons.

★ TEACHER TIP
Students should also be encouraged to narrate using Legos, puppets, or by acting out the scene.

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (Canada) - Lesson 23
lessons.body:
Métis Culture and the Buffalo Hunt, cont.; Catch-up Day
⍞ Materials: The Gift of the Great Buffalo

➜ PREP
Read the "Author's Note" section (the last two pages of the book) and decide how you would like to share any of this information with your student(s). If you have read any of the Little House books, comparing the two stories could be a good point of discussion.

➜ CATCH UP
Use this time to catch up on lessons in this or another course, or explore Extra Helpings.

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (Canada) - Lesson 25
lessons.body:
Names
⍞ Materials: The Honour Drum

PREP: Read p.21 Discussion Question #2 in advance and decide how best to contextualize it for your own family and community.

➜ INTRO
How do you introduce yourself when you meet someone new? There are many different terms and names used for Indigenous peoples, and in today's reading, we will learn more about what they mean.

➜ VIEW
∞ Video Link: The word Indigenous

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.20-21 "The words we use" - "tied at waist level."

• Questions #1-2 on p.21 can guide your discussion of the reading.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• EXTRA HELPING
Try a lovely weaving activity, perfect for little hands! All you need is some yarn and a twig from a nature walk.
∞ Video Link: Twig Weaving (4:48)

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (Canada) - Lesson 26
lessons.body:
The Iroquois Confederacy
⍞ Materials: Hiawatha and the Peacemakers; a globe or map of North America

PREP: Read "Historical Note" on p.43.

➜ INTRO
Point to the Great Lakes on a map or globe. This story takes place on the eastern shores, where five Tribes were constantly at war with one another.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS 
p.1-10 "A fierce scream" - "great Peacemaker."
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER TIP
Remember to recap the previous lesson before starting the new lesson.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.11-18 "I stared at" - "as two nations."
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER TIP
Students should also be encouraged to narrate using Legos, puppets, or by acting out the scene.

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (Canada) - Lesson 28
lessons.body:
The Iroquois Confederacy, cont. 
⍞ Materials: Hiawatha and the Peacemakers

Can you remember what was happening when we left off?

➜ INTRO

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.19-28 "Guided by the moon" - "as four nations."
lessons.teacherNotes: • ILLUSTRATE - Draw a scene from the story using watercolor or another medium such as chalk, pastels, or crayons.

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (Canada) - Lesson 29

The Iroquois Confederacy, cont.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.29-36 "The wicked" - "where they belonged."

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (Canada) - Lesson 30
lessons.body:
The Iroquois Confederacy, cont.
⍞ Materials: Hiawatha and the Peacemakers

➜ RECAP
Can you remember what was happening when we left off?

➜ READ & NARRATE
p.37-42 "Everyone followed" - "tree of peace."

➜ DISCUSS
Share a little from the Historical Note on p.43, particularly that the Iroquois Confederacy influenced the United States' Founding Fathers when they were setting up their government.
lessons.teacherNotes: • ILLUSTRATE - Draw a scene from the story using watercolor or another medium such as chalk, pastels, or crayons.

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (Canada) - Lesson 31
lessons.body:
The Creator
⍞ Materials: The Honour Drum

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.22-23 "Indigenous people" - "away from ourselves."

• Questions #1-2 on p.23 can guide your discussion of the reading.

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (Canada) - Lesson 32
lessons.body:
The Land
⍞ Materials: The Honour Drum; On the Trapline

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.24-25 "We honour Creator" - "blankets and shelter."

• Questions #1-3 on p.25 can guide your discussion of the reading.

➜ INTRO
On the Trapline is the story of a grandfather who travels with his grandson back to the northern land where he lived long ago as a child. Our reading from The Honour Drum today describes how Indigenous people feel a strong tie to their traditional land, and in On the Trapline, we will follow a grandfather sharing this bond and culture with his young grandson.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
"I'm on my way up north" - "Kapasimo means 'swim.'"
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER TIP
There is a glossary at the back of the book with a guide to pronouncing the Swampy Cree vocabulary.

• EXTRA HELPING
Explore a map of Canada showing Indigenous place names (with translations). For best viewing, download a PDF and zoom in on your computer.
∞ Map Link: Indigenous Place Names Map of Canada

• NATURE NOTEBOOK
Go on a nature walk this week to observe God's creation all around you. What is happening in nature right now? Do you see any signs of changing seasons?

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (Canada) - Lesson 33
lessons.body:
On the Trapline, cont.
⍞ Materials: On the Trapline

➜ INTRO
What do you like to do when you spend time with your grandparents or other older adults in your life? Are there any special stories or songs they like to share?

➜ RECAP
What do you remember from what we read last time?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
"We drive down" - "Pisiskowak means 'animals.'"
lessons.teacherNotes: • ILLUSTRATE - Draw a scene from the story using watercolor or another medium such as chalk, pastels, or crayons.

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (Canada) - Lesson 34
lessons.body:
On the Trapline, cont.
⍞ Materials: On the Trapline; The Honour Drum

➜ RECAP
What do you remember from what we read last time?

➜ READ & NARRATE 
"When we're about to leave" - "I can see it too."

➜ DISCUSS
Think about Moshom's life as a child. How was his childhood different from his grandson's? How was it different from yours?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.26-27 "Imagine if there were" - "at all times."

• Questions #1-3 on p.27 can guide your discussion of the reading.
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER TIP
Students should also be encouraged to narrate using Legos, puppets, or by acting out the scene.

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (Canada) - Lesson 35
lessons.body:
Listening and Learning More
⍞ Materials: The Honour Drum

➜ READ & NARRATE
p.28-29 "To listen" - "Truth and Reconciliation."

➜ DISCUSS
Thinking about the books you've read this year, can you remember any times when characters listened to each other? Or didn't listen? Do you remember times when characters were silent?

➜ READ & NARRATE
Now that we've reached the end of the book, return to p.1 and read the entire poem "The Honour Drum" from start to finish.

➜ DISCUSS
Questions #1-3 on p.29 can guide your discussion of what you have learned.
Also, return to Questions #2-3 on p.3. Now that you have learned about Indigenous culture in Canada, research more about Indigenous communities near you and about which community's traditional land is where you live now. The link below can help.

➜ VIEW
∞ Map Link: Native Land Digital

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (Canada) - Lesson 36
lessons.body:
Exam Week

➜ EXAMS
Answer question(s) related to the course. If a book was not finished during the term, adjust the exam questions to reflect what was read or allow students to tell about or draw from stories or excerpts that they remember. If a student has difficulty getting started or gets "stuck," they may look back at illustrations to help them refocus on ideas.

Questions will come from:
On the Trapline
Hiawatha and the Peacemaker

terms.content: · (The Pencil) Describe what life was like for Susan Avingaq growing up in the Arctic. OR Tell about or draw the story of The Owl and the Lemming.

· (Maple Moon) Tell about or draw the story of how Rides the Wind discovered sweet water from a maple tree. OR (The Gift of the Great Buffalo) Tell all you know about Métis culture and their community buffalo hunts.
· (On the Trapline) Tell about or illustrate the boy's journey with Moshom, his grandfather. OR (Hiawatha and the Peacemaker) Tell about or illustrate the story of Hiawatha and the Peacemaker.
